title: Ordering at the café
setting: You walk into a Lucerne café for breakfast. The server greets you.

options.es: Grüezi. Ich hätt gärn es Kafi, bitte.
options.en: Hello. I'd like a coffee, please.
options.feedback: Perfect — 'ich hätt gärn' is the polite way to order. Returning 'Grüezi' first is exactly right.
Understandable, but blunt. A 'Grüezi' and 'bitte' go a long way in Switzerland.
Wrong language! Here it's 'es Kafi, bitte.'

steps.speaker: Serviererin
steps.en: Gladly. And something to eat with it?

options.feedback: Spot on — a Gipfeli with coffee is the classic Swiss breakfast.
Perfectly polite. 'Nu' (just/only) is a handy little word.
Fair to ask! It's a croissant. Curiosity is fine when you're learning.

options.en: Here you go. Thank you very much.
options.feedback: Smooth and warm — 'merci viilmal' is everyday Lucerne politeness.
Okay if you didn't catch it, but she just said it — try to listen for 'Stutz' (francs).
Haggling isn't a thing in Swiss cafés — prices are fixed. Just pay and smile.
Perfect — 'glichfalls' (likewise) is exactly how a local returns the wish.
Friendly and natural. A simple 'Tschüss' works well.
A bit flat for her warm send-off. Return the kindness with 'glichfalls'.
